target_emits.name: create_layer2_vlan_shared
target_emits.purpose: Create the underlying L2 VLAN profile in the Library for each VLAN ID referenced by the binding. Required because Central will not auto-create the layer2-vlan profile when the named-VLAN -> alias chain resolves to a VLAN ID.
target_emits.iteration_notes: If source vlan-ids = '108-110', emit step 1 three times — once each for VLAN 108, 109, 110. Range expansion happens here because Central's layer2-vlan profile-instance is a single integer ID per profile.

target_emits.name: create_alias_shared
target_emits.purpose: Create the alias profile in the Library with a placeholder VLAN ID. Real value(s) arrive at step 6 via a LOCAL override at the deployment scope.

target_emits.name: create_named_vlan_shared
target_emits.purpose: Create the named-VLAN profile in the Library that references the alias by name. The named-VLAN ALWAYS uses vlan-alias in this translation pattern; vlan-id-ranges on the named-VLAN body is a separate Central use case (VLAN groups) and not what this translation is for.

target_emits.name: assign_layer2_vlan
target_emits.purpose: Assign the L2 VLAN profile(s) to the target scope. Multiple device-functions are packed into a single POST per VLAN ID.

config-assignment: {one entry per device_function in target_meta.device_functions: { scope-id, device-function, profile-type='layer2-vlan', profile-instance='{vlan_id}' }}

target_emits.iteration_notes: One POST per VLAN ID; all device-functions share the array.

target_emits.name: assign_named_vlan
target_emits.purpose: Assign the named-VLAN profile to the target scope. Multiple device-functions packed into a single POST. Central AUTO-PULLS the referenced alias to the same scope + same device-function set behind the scenes — no explicit alias assignment needed (verified empirically; see draft_notes).

config-assignment: {one entry per device_function: { scope-id, device-function, profile-type='named-vlan', profile-instance='{vlan_name}' }}

target_emits.iteration_notes: One POST; all device-functions share the array.

target_emits.name: override_alias_local
target_emits.purpose: Create a LOCAL override of the alias at the target scope with the real VLAN ID(s). This is what makes the named-VLAN actually resolve to the correct VLANs at the deployment scope without dirtying the Library default.
target_emits.iteration_notes: Step 6 is per (scope, device-function) — the LOCAL override path takes scope-id + device-function as query params, not as an array (unlike config-assignments). One POST per device-function with the same body.

target_meta.device_function_filtering_rule: Steps 1-3 are SHARED creates with no device-function param. Steps 4 + 5 use the config-assignments array shape and pack all applicable device-functions into one POST per profile per VLAN ID. Step 6 is per device-function (LOCAL override path takes scope-id + device-function as query params, not as an array). Operator can configure which device functions apply per session by overriding device_functions in runtime_values.

target_scope_id_resolution.rule: Central scope_id for emits 4-6 is supplied by the consuming skill at runtime, derived from the source binding scope (e.g. for the aos-migration skill: AOS 8 vlan_name_id binding scope -> Central scope_id via Stage 7 hierarchy mapping).
target_scope_id_resolution.input: Source binding scope (string path / identifier); skill-specific

alias_name.rationale: Operator-stated convention: alias is always the lower-case form of the named-VLAN name, mirroring the AOS 8 CLI form 'vlan user-vlan 104' that operators are familiar with. Pure target-side rule, source-independent — applies regardless of which source platform produced the named-VLAN name.

depended_by.notes: When a source's virtual_ap.vlan.vlan is a string matching a 'name' from this translation (e.g., 'user'), the WLAN translation references the Central named-vlan by name. When it's a numeric string (e.g., '107'), the WLAN translation goes through the bare layer2-vlan path instead. Downstream WLAN/SSID translation needs to disambiguate.

objects.notes: Registers the symbolic name at a scope. Carries no VLAN-ID information. Inheritance: copies appear at descendant scopes with _flags.inherited=true; filter those out (only definition entries contribute).

Binds a registered name to one or more VLAN IDs. Field 'vlan-ids' is a comma-separated string where each element is either an ID ('107') or a range ('108-110'). Live-verified that AOS 8 PRESERVES range syntax in this object (unlike the bare vlan_id object which expands ranges).

merge_rule.description: For each unique 'name' across both source objects, find the deepest (most-specific) scope where a vlan_name_id binding exists. Skip names registered via vlan_name with no corresponding vlan_name_id anywhere in the hierarchy.
merge_rule.inheritance_handling: Drop entries with _flags.inherited == True from BOTH source objects before merge. The canonical record is the (scope, name) pair where the binding was authored, not the inherited copies.

vlan_name.to: vlan_name (Central path param + body 'name' on step 3, profile-instance on step 5)
vlan_name.notes: AOS 8 'name' value becomes Central's named-vlan name. Operator can override per session (live observation: existing tenants often use upper-case '-VLAN' suffix style like USER-VLAN / IOT-VLAN, but operators don't always follow it).

vlan_ids.to: vlan-id-ranges (alias body, step 6) AND profile-instance (layer2-vlan creation step 1, assignment step 4)
vlan_ids.notes: Asymmetric transform: step 1 + step 4 EXPAND ranges to discrete IDs (one layer2-vlan profile per ID; Central's profile-instance is a single integer); step 6 PRESERVES the range string in the alias body (Central's vlan-id-ranges accepts 'x-y' syntax directly per the schema regex).

unmapped_fields.reason: AOS 8 lets operators register a symbolic name without ever binding it to a VLAN ID. Central's named-vlan profile must resolve to either a vlan-alias or vlan-id-ranges (per ArubaNamedVlan_NamedVlanprofileSchema); a profile that resolves to nothing is non-functional. Skip the entire 6-emit chain for these entries.

ignored_variants.reason: Separate AOS 8 REST object for VLAN pool / VLAN-grp configuration. Out of scope for this translation — gets its own sibling translation (vlan_pool_v1.json, not yet authored).

draft_notes: Verified empirically (2026-05-07) against the live Central tenant: SHARED-only POST creates a Library orphan with no scope assignments; LOCAL-only POST does not auto-create a Library entry; SHARED + scope-id is rejected with HTTP 400 'For shared objects, do not provide scope id and/or persona'. Translation MUST do the explicit 6-step flow above.

Verified empirically: config-assignments POST accepts a multi-element array — single POST with [{MOBILITY_GW}, {CAMPUS_AP}] entries returned 200 success and both assignments were visible via GET /config-assignments. Steps 4 + 5 use this packing.

Verified empirically (2026-05-07): assigning a SHARED named-VLAN profile to a scope/device-function set AUTO-PULLS the referenced alias to the same scope + same device-function set. Step 5 (explicit alias assignment) was therefore dropped from the original 7-step flow as redundant.

Verified the SHARED vs LOCAL distinction via aruba-annotation:object_type metadata on GET responses: SHARED at GLOBAL with default placeholder; LOCAL at scope with overridden_scope='GLOBAL' tag. The same body shape (name + type + default-value) is used for both; only request context (query params + scope) determines which type Central creates.

Verified AOS 8 vlan_name_id PRESERVES range syntax in vlan-ids ('108-110' for the 'iot' name). The bare vlan_id object EXPANDS ranges to discrete entries. So step 6 alias-override carries the range string verbatim while step 1/4 iterate over expanded discrete IDs.

The named-vlan body uses ONLY vlan-alias in this translation pattern. Central's named-VLAN schema also supports vlan-id-ranges directly (without an alias), but that's a separate use case (VLAN groups) and not what this translation produces — every named-VLAN we emit goes through the alias-first pattern so values can be overridden per scope without modifying the named-VLAN profile itself.
